removing the custom aws api listeners -> :star_struck: 

my biggest concern currently is change to the serializer API, heres some high-level feedback:

* conceptually it seems odd to have to pass aspects of the _request_ (the request headers) to serialize a _response_, maybe we could make this more high-level (ideas below).
* moreover, we now have multiple control paths in the same serializer dealing with different output formats. again, conceptually it seems cleaner to have specific serializer instances for specific output formats, rather than hiding the output format inside a single serializer.

is there anything that speaks against having the skeleton decide which serializer to use based on the accept header of the requests? it seems to me that the caller of the serializer should be responsible for deciding what the output format should be, not the serializer.

In Chrome if one is starting the setup wizard for the first time with no cookies/cached data from previous interaction with Kolibri the browser's back button is disabled which effectively forces the user to use the 'Go back' button and that's fine. 
Still I was able to replicate the issue observed by @radinamatic by instead selecting a valid Kolibri link saved as a bookmark. In that case the user sees the 'You must be signed in to view this page" message and after clicking the link to the sign in page is redirected to the Setup Wizard with enabled Back button:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/assets/79847249/857c07a8-925e-4690-ac9a-6bcc00eaaee6

In Firefox the browser's back button is enabled which results in weird behavior, and also allows me to go back to the Setup Wizard after having completed the setup process:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/assets/79847249/b662664a-bcc7-41d6-844c-2c6f63354f3e

<del>Wanted to test this in Internet Explorer 11 as well, but the wizard was not working at all there so it's best to check if you have code which is not supported by old browsers.</del> - was some caching issue on my end I guess, works fine in IE11

Rather than changing the signature of `ray.init`, can we follow the pattern of  how `_credentials` is passed:

Something like the following would get `_metadata` to the `connect` call. 
```
diff --git a/python/ray/client_builder.py b/python/ray/client_builder.py
index 51ccffd38c..a25db0ad6e 100644
--- a/python/ray/client_builder.py
+++ b/python/ray/client_builder.py
@@ -116,6 +116,7 @@ class ClientBuilder:
         # Set to False if ClientBuilder is being constructed by internal
         # methods
         self._deprecation_warn_enabled = True
+        self._metadata = None
 
     def env(self, env: Dict[str, Any]) -> "ClientBuilder":
         """
@@ -179,6 +180,7 @@ class ClientBuilder:
             job_config=self._job_config,
             _credentials=self._credentials,
             ray_init_kwargs=self._remote_init_kwargs,
+            metadata=self._metadata,
         )
         get_dashboard_url = ray.remote(ray._private.worker.get_dashboard_url)
         dashboard_url = ray.get(get_dashboard_url.options(num_cpus=0).remote())
@@ -229,6 +231,10 @@ class ClientBuilder:
             self._credentials = kwargs["_credentials"]
             del kwargs["_credentials"]
 
+        if "_metadata" in kwargs.keys():
+            self._metadata = kwargs["_metadata"]
+            del kwargs["_metadata"]
+
         if kwargs:
             expected_sig = inspect.signature(ray_driver_init)
             extra_args = set(kwargs.keys()).difference(expected_sig.parameters.keys())
```

Wow, @DaelonSuzuka , that's indeed a lot of work—thanks! Its a sufficiently big extension of QtPy (with many new modules, new errors, and other changes) that I think it makes sense to ship it in 1.2, assuming @dalthviz agrees.

I highly recommend applying my suggestions first, which are generally straightforward and uncontroversial, before addressing anything else, assuming you don't disagree with them; otherwise, they are likely to get outdated/lost.

Some high-level feedback and suggestions:

* [ ] Right now, since QtPy is still using the implicit ("flat") package layout rather than the now-recommended explicit `src` layout, this means that `module_report` is a top-level import package equal to `qtpy` itself and will be included in the distribution package and installed in the user's environment as a separate import package, which is of course not what we want. We could exclude it with some futzing in the setuptools configuration, but IMO its still confusing to have two top-level import packages, one of which is just a development-focused helper tool. 

  Therefore, after consultation with you and @dalthviz , I suggest we do one of the following:
  
  * Stick `module_report` inside a `tools` directory (which wouldn't be importable and is ignored by default in the new Setuptools 61 auto-discovery) and run it from there when needed *(simplest solution to the immediate problem)*
  * If we think this functionality is likely to be useful to other developers using QtPy to check their environments, we _could_ add it as a subpackage of the actual `qtpy` package, and add it as a command to the QtPy development CLI. *(Somewhat nicer usability for us and allows other devs to use it to, if that has value, though a little more effort and means it is part of QtPy itself)*
  * Make `module_report` its own separate repo/distribution package (Better usability for us and others without bundling it in QtPy itself, and if you want to help maintain it, you could much more easily be added as a Collaborator on that repo, but is more initial work, more overhead and less directly coupled to QtPy's own development)

  Making it a separate repo, installable (at least) from GitHub, is the "nicest" solution, but also the most work, up front and (to some extent) over time, so whether this is viable depends on whether you want to help maintain it, and also on if it more generally useful to application developers rather than just development of QtPy itself. Making it a subpackage of `qtpy` buys us the latter benefit, if it has significant value, with less work, but also means that it gets shipped to all users, few of which are likely to need it.
  
  So IMO, just keeping it in a `tools` directory is probably the best approach for now unless and until those other conditions are a compelling enough reason to do otherwise (which we could always do in the future). 

* [ ] If we're going to check the output files into the repo, we should run `module_report` in our CIs to catch any changes and either update the files or take other action accordingly. However, given the results can vary between OSes (e.g. `DBus` on Windows) and distributions (`pip`/`conda`), it might be better to just not check them in directly, or if so make the checked-in files specifically for comparison to a per-platform CI check that determines if the output matches the expected (but that latter part would be *optional* for this PR). This could also help automate and decouple the testing from the details of the implementation, which you brought up some valid concerns about—but again, that could go in a future PR.

* [ ] Instead of making all the exception messages base `PythonQtError`s (such that there's no way for tools to programmatically determine whether the module was not installed, not supported on that binding, or some other error occurred) and writing manual messages for each one (which is way more work to add, check and change, and easier to make a typo on), we should subclass it with specific errors of each type, that take a `name` and a `binding` argument (with a `msg` override) and construct the message from there. This also allows us to have it inherit the built-in `ModuleNotFoundError` so it behaves as expected.
  
    Therefore, I suggest adding the following to the `__init__.py` under the existing errors and using them accordingly:

    ```python
     class QtModuleNotFoundError(ModuleNotFoundError, PythonQtError):
        """Raised when a Python Qt binding submodule is not installed/supported."""
        _msg = 'The {name} module was not found for {binding}.'
        _msg_extra = ''

        def __init__(self, *, name, binding=None, msg=None, **msg_kwargs):
            self.binding = binding
            msg = msg or f'{self._msg} {self._msg_extra}'.strip()
            msg = msg.format(name=name, binding=binding, **msg_kwargs)
            super().__init__(msg, name=name)

    class QtBindingMissingModuleError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raised when a module is not supported by a given binding."""
        _msg_extra = 'It is not currently implemented in {binding}.'

    class QtModuleNotInstalledError(QtModuleNotFoundError):
        """Raise when a module is supported by the binding, but not installed."""
        _msg_extra = 'It must be installed separately'

        def __init__(self, *, missing_package=None, **superclass_kwargs):
            self.missing_package = missing_package
            if missing_package is not None:
                 self._msg_extra += ' as {missing_package}.'
            super().__init__(missing_package=missing_package, **superclass_kwargs)
    ```

Also, a few smaller but common issues (which I made suggestions to fix, except where noted):

* [ ] Let's please not use `*` imports in `module_report`, as they are considered a very bad practice and make the code hard to understand, introspect and lint, among other reasons. It is true that QtPy uses them internally, but for a very specific purpose—ensuring it picks up the full namespace of the packages it wraps without hardcoding—that isn't the case here. Also, we should use standard, conventional PEP 8 import ordering.
* [ ] Module-level constants are ALL_CAPS per PEP 8 and standard convention
* [ ] Don't forget to add terminating EOLs to the files
* [ ] Per PEP 257, docstrings must end with periods and one-line docstrings should be on one line (as they are currently), not with `'''"` above and below (I didn't make review comments for this to avoid noise)

> One option we have to fix those that would not require an API-breaking change would be to make a deep copy of the in-progress Package at each call to Snapshot. This would break pointer equality all over the place, but would at least ensure that we're not tip-toeing around data races.

Even with this option, we still leak the in-progress `*Package` via the iterators, etc. With that in mind, I think that we should merge this as-is, tolerate the data race for the moment (which only arises if a consumer is reading one of the racy fields at the same time it is being published or if the language importer is running while a consumer is reading from language-specific data), and plan on making a breaking change to the APIs that expose the raw `*Package`. Imagine Mallory gets a signing certificate similar to MS's signing certificate, from some recognized CA.  They create a cert/key and sign the cert with the signing certificate.  Then they create an attested document containing a malicious vmID, a signature made with the key, and the cert.  And hand it to us.

1. ✔️ The embedded document was signed with the private key corresponding to the embedded public key
2. ✔️ The embedded public key has a proper certificate chain back to a trusted CA
3. ✔️ The embedded message contains the vmId that matches the worker making the request

So basically we've verified that someone signed the message and that the PKI infrastructure can tell us who that someone is -- but we don't actually check that it was Microsoft.

1. (preferred) Update the checkpoint to allow storing the 'cursor' value
2. Use our knowledge of the internals of the API create a new 'DateCursorPaginator' which extracts the date and doc ID from the cursor to store in the checkpoint.

@ericl @jianoaix In general, consistently resetting the index was a very early design decision, where we decided that a block's index should represent the dataset record order. This means that we need to reset the index whenever we slice, concatenate, or shuffle blocks, with the end-result being that each block is locally ordered, and if all blocks are concatenated together (e.g. via `ds.to_pandas()`), the resulting index would give the global dataset order. This was under the assumption that the user would _not_ be encoding application-level identifiers into the index, since we hadn't encountered that use case at the time, and this was a bit of a pragmatic choice, since trying to maintain a sensible index on a distributed DataFrame is a bit difficult, and would require a lot of care to ensure that accidentally-aligned indices wouldn't yield unintended outputs on certain Pandas operations.

@jianoaix This particular index drop was added because of this issue: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/27019/commits/a48e34d25477f4d0f90b7bb7ac4909c8a042b696#r931308133.

If we're going to try to maintain the index, we can't only make the change on concatenation; we would need to do a global audit of all other places we reset the index, i.e. slicing, taking, shuffling:
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L147
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L154
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L167

And before we decide to try to maintain the index, we need to ensure that index-aware operations (i.e. operations that automatically align on indices) will not now give incorrect results, and we might want to only preserve the original index if the index is not the default sequential ordering index (assuming there's an easy way to check that).

As it is, you could do this:

```
>>> n1, n2, n3 = [ three whole Notes ]
>>> sl = spanner.Slur([n1, n2])
>>> s.append([timeSignature, sl, n1, n2, n3])
>>> s.makeMeasures(inPlace=True)
>>> sf = s.flat
>>> sf.remove(sl)  # slur is gone
>>> s2 = sf.makeMeasures()
>>> excerpt = s2.measures(1, 2)
>>> sl in excerpt  # True!
```

Tests should go into `test_trial_scheduler_pbt.py`. You can just add a new case in `PopulationBasedTrainingSynchTest`.

My suggestion would be to do something like

```
pbt = PopulationBasedTraining(sync=True, burn_in_period=6, perturbation_interval=2)
runner = _MockTrialRunner(pbt)

# Create some mock trials
trial1 = ...

runner.add_trial(trial1)
runner.add_trial(trial2)
runner.add_trial(trial3)
runner.add_trial(trial4)

# Report some results just before burn in period
pbt.on_trial_result(runner, trial1, {"metric": 4})
...

# assert that we don't continue training, yet
self.assertFalse(pbt.choose_trial_to_run())

# Report one more resul
pbt.on_trial_result(runner, trial1, {"metric": 4})

# Assert all trials are paused
self.assertTrue(all(t.status == "PAUSED" for t in [trial1, trial2, trial3, trial4]))

# assert that we continue training now
self.assertTrue(pbt.choose_trial_to_run())
```

etc, so no full end-to-end test, just unit testing the pbt decisions

OK, here we go! I'll start by repeating what I said above. This is very high quality contribution! Thanks for taking the time to research and understand the issue thoroughly, and then posting a high quality solution! I've learnt a lot reviewing and checking the background context needed. Thanks again!

You'll see plenty of comments, but in general the main points are:
* The `context` object might not need the `is_prelease` method there, since a check for the PackageRecord, MatchSpec or VersionOrder objects might be enough.
* I am concerned about the ramifications of the `limit` mode, which is [the default mode suggested by PEP440](https://peps.python.org/pep-0440/#handling-of-pre-releases). If we implement this, we need to decide how we are going to interpret the PEP440 directives, specially when it comes to the "allow pre-releases for packages already using a pre-release" directive, if we understand it in a package-by-package basis. This of course would need specific tests.
* How we handle `*`-containing versions.

I am also curious about what happens to projects that are _only_ available as pre-releases (for now). The most popular example is `black`. I think `pip` handles this with an exception, but it still begs the question: should a prerelease-only project be always available regardless the `prerelease=xxxx` option chosen? PEP440 seems to be addressing this in the 'limit'  mode implementation but, again, we need tests for that too.

On top of this, I checked open issues in `pypa/pip` and other tools, to see what shortcomings the community might have found before us. Please take a look at the following references and let's see if we can establish whether the current status of the PR suffers from the same issue(s):

* Intersection with other flags and/or commands? https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/8983
* This part of the PEP440 'limit' mode: https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/9121
* This other issue about automated opt-in via the specifier. It sounds like pip enables RCs if the specifier mentions RCs, but it shoudl only mean so for the current stable branch, not every subsequent RC? https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/7579

Last but not least, a ramification of this feature. Allowing prereleases has effects on two levels: creating the index and solving the environment. On the index creation side, it determines the availability of the package. The solving side means whether we pick it or not. In that vein, wonder if we should extend the availability checks to `conda search`. 

Apologies for the length of the review! This is an extremely useful and important feature, and I am very grateful we are progressing in the right direction. Thanks again for the nth time :)

2. I worry a bit about the list of domains becoming too long to shove in the OS environment. This only changes type annotations with one small exception, which is a change in variable names in well tested code path, this change in variable names is needed for type checking to pass without error/ignores as mypy does not like that the same variable has different types in the same scope:

https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/06feef03ec071a265f79ea08d95b91be62ea2f70/rdflib/graph.py#L495-L497

This PR also narrows some type annotations, but this narrowing brings things more in line with RDF 1.1 spec so I think it is good, if someone wants to use rdflib with non compliant types and type checking they will have to use `# type: ignore` or `cast` - but on the other hand, if we try and cater for non compliant RDF it also makes our lives harder.

The narrower types should however help users of this library detect errors in their code where they are potentially creating invalid RDF graphs.

I will add a page in docs regarding type annotations which covers some of the questions in #1696 and #1447

I'm imagining that we would send them to either based on what they are asking for:
- if they know what they are doing and they just want to see how to use the api, we'd send them to [the one of the concept page](https://docs.dagster.io/concepts/ops-jobs-graphs/jobs-graphs#conditional-branching) for "api usage"
- if they bring their use case and ask for recommendations (which has been most of questions that we ended up answering with conditional branching), we'd send them to this example for "here's an example for accomplishing a similar use case + how you can connect dots among multiple dagster concepts". 

in other words, i view the current examples on concept pages as api usage while this example would focus more on the "why"s and using 2+ dagster concepts (i.e. more of a dagster project that can actually launch runs, as opposed to small code snippets).
